Municipal Accountant maintain their own
inventory records of medical, dental, and
laboratory supplies and welfare goods and
drugs and medicines and correctly record
items consumed and/or distributed during
the year and properly account for the
remaining items on hand if any; and (b)
BAC and concerned officials of the
agency comply with the requirements
under the 2016 Revised IRRs of R.A. No.
9184 and COA Circular No. 2012-001
and submit the lacking documentary
requirements to the Audit Team to prove
the propriety and regularity of
disbursement transactions noted.

We recommended that the: (a) members Fully
of the Local Finance Committee continue Implemented
to ensure that the required appropriation
for local development fund is always
observed; (b) Municipal Development
Council continue to comply the guidelines
mandated by DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No.

Further, we recommended that Fully
Management return the unused balance of Implemented
fund transfers to the source agencies in

1994.

We recommended that the Management Fully
hasten the preparation of the documentary Implemented
requirements prescribed under DBM LBC
No. 142 to begin the implementation of
the projects before the set deadline and
ensure the efficient delivery of public
service to the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that the: (a) members Fully
of the Local Finance Committee ensure Implemented
that the 5% minimum required
appropriation for GAD is always
observed; (b) GAD Focal Point System (i) Fully
continue the complete implementation of Implemented
GAD PPAs to maximize benefits inuring
to the beneficiaries; and (ii) take the lead Not Implemented Reiterated in this
and communicate with the Department of report

Provide additional manpower, when feasible, to assist in the preparation and reconciliation of PPE records and reports;
Strictly follow the recording of a tangible item as PPE, if and only if, it meets the definition and recognition criteria for PPE, which includes the capitalization threshold of ₱50,000.00.

Require the Inventory Committee, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, to:
Prioritize the preparation of the approved Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) and submit it to the Office of the Auditor, at least ten (10) calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory-taking activities; and

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct:
a.	The Municipal Treasurer to:
Ensure accuracy and completeness in the preparation of the Report of Checks Issued in accordance with Section 43 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs; and
ii.	Prepare and provide a copy of the list of unreleased checks to the Municipal Accountant at the end of the year.
b.	The Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjustments for unreleased checks at year-end, to restore the Cash in Bank and corresponding Payable accounts in the Financial Statements.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct:
The Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Municipal Assessor, to prepare and furnish the Municipal Accountant at the beginning of the year with a duly certified list of taxpayers showing the amount due and collectible for the year;

The Municipal Accountant to:
Recognize the year-end balance of the receivable accounts in the books based on the Certified List of Real Property Tax Delinquencies report from the Municipal Treasurer;

Coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer for the timely submission of the certified list of taxpayers and eventual recognition in the books at the beginning of the calendar year of the Real Property Tax/Special Education Tax Receivables and related Deferred accounts, in accordance with Section 20 of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Volume I for LGU; and
The Municipal Treasurer to create a more aggressive and intensified campaign for the collection of all RPT and SET Receivables and consequently, to possibly minimize uncollected accounts.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct:
The Municipal Accountant to follow the Perpetual Inventory Method in the accounting of inventories in compliance with Section 114 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for LGUs Volume I; and

The GSO to ensure that issuance of supplies and materials are supported with Requisition and Issue Slip, and prepare and submit the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued to the Municipal Accountant for the recording of expense.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Accountant to:
Expedite the reconciliation of the documents relative to the abnormal/negative balances in the subsidiary ledgers of the Due to NGAs and Due to LGUs accounts with a total debit balance of ₱7.986 million; and

Prepare the necessary adjusting entries in accordance with Item 7.3 of COA Circular No. 2016-005, to reflect in the financial statements the correct balance of the Due to NGAs and Due to LGUs accounts

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
 Ensure that only properties that are considered useful and practicable, especially for intended beneficiaries, are purchased as planned to avoid losses from deterioration of idle PPE;
Create a Disposal Committee in accordance with Section 79 of P.D. No. 1445, to determine the serviceability of PPEs and facilitate the disposal process of the unserviceable properties;

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Exhaust efforts to retrieve the supporting documents and expedite the return of the unexpended balances to the Source Agencies in accordance with COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994; and

Coordinate with the concerned implementing offices upon receipt of funds transferred from various NGAs and LGUs, for the timely implementation of the said offices of the programs, projects and activities, and prompt recording of its subsequent liquidation.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct:
The Accountant to:
Require completeness of the attached DTTs for fuel disbursements; and
Ensure consistency of data between the Charge Invoices and Statements of Account, Charge Invoices and Gas Tickets, and Gas Tickets and DTTs.

The Municipal Treasurer to ensure that all disbursements are duly supported with proof of payment such as official receipt or its equivalent document, that should contain complete details of payment including the name of payee, date of payment, particulars and amount; and

The Responsible Officer, in accordance with COA Circular No. 77-61, dated September 26, 1977, to:
Ensure that Driver’s Trip Tickets are approved, properly accomplished, serially numbered, and duly signed by the driver and the passenger;

Prepare the Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption in accordance with the format prescribed in Annex G of the Manual on Audit for Fuel Consumption of Government Vehicles and submit to the Office of the Auditor; and
Prepare the Monthly Report of Official Travels and submit to the Office of the Auditor.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct:
The Accountant to devise ways and means in order to prepare the necessary reports readily and accurately, without resorting to misclassification of the expenses;
Instruct the concerned offices, when necessary, to provide a specific budget for Fuel, Oil and Lubricants, otherwise, undergo the correct process of budget augmentation; and
Require the Accountant to stop the practice of recording the fuel expenses in accounts other than Fuel, Oil and Lubricants to strictly adhere to COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the designated Property Officer to accomplish and file the Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE) form for all of the PPE issued in accordance with Section 123, Chapter 7 of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual, in order to determine the accountable persons and establish accountability.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the 
Municipal Accountant to: 
Exhaust all efforts to retrieve the supporting documents relative to the long outstanding book reconciling items of the General and Trust Funds; and

Cause the immediate preparation of the journal entries to record the reconciling items that require adjustment and correction on the GL in accordance with Section 3.3 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant:
To prepare the bank reconciliation statements for the Bottom Up Budgeting and KALAHI Trust Fund Accounts in order to determine the reconciling items for the net difference of ₱27,859.15 between the book balance and the bank confirmation balance, in compliance with Section 74 of P.D. No. 1445 and Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 6, 1996;

To prepare journal entry vouchers to record all valid reconciling items that require adjustment and correction in the GL consistent with Section 3.3 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 6, 1996; and

To submit the copies of the BRS to the Auditor within ten (10) days from receipt of BS after the end of each month, including the paid checks, original copies of the debit and credit memo in accordance with Section 3.4 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 6, 1996.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the MDRRMO to ensure strict compliance with Section 4 of NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 on the utilization of the DRRMF.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct:
The MDRRMO, together with the Municipal Development Council, to review and update the LDRRMFIP annually to include a list of specific projects and activities to be charged against the LDRRMF of prior years and incorporate it in the AIP; and
The OIC-Municipal Accountant to ensure that the PPAs are included in the AIP prior to the processing of disbursements related to its implementation.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting journal entries and comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 by following the prescribed accounting entries in order to come up with a more accurate presentation of the Financial Statements.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct: 
The concerned Rural Health Unit personnel to prepare the Inventory Report on the remaining undistributed vaccines as of year-end with information as to valuation and furnish the copy to the Office of the Municipal Accountant; and
The Municipal Accountant to record the transaction on the agency books based on the complete Inventory Report in compliance with COA Circular No. 2020-009.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Accountable Officer to stamp “PAID” the Disbursement Vouchers (DVs) and all the supporting documents attached to the DVs after the payment is made, to prevent re-use of the said documents for similar transactions.

We reiterated, and the management agreed on the following recommendations: 
The Local Chief Executive to direct the concerned employees or designate personnel to gather the necessary papers/documents and facilitate the transfer of ownership of the donated vehicles to the Municipality; and

The Inventory Committee to conduct an inventory of all the parcels of land owned by the Municipality consistent with Section 5.1 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 and reconcile with the records in the books; and

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the concerned officials and employees to communicate with the MLGOO and DILG Regional Office regarding the Copy of the List of Eligible LGUs and the Notice of Eligibility;
We recommended that management cause the refund of the excess of the 2013 PEI in the total amount of ₱1,106,000.00.

We recommended strict adherence to the provision of Section 9.1 and 9.2 of Budget Circular No. 2012-3. The amount of ₱1,856,000.00, being unauthorized should be refunded by the municipal officials and employees who were recipients of the benefits.

We recommended that management: a. Enjoin the Inventory Committee to conduct the inventory taking and thereafter prepare and submit the following to the Audit Team in the prescribed format: i. Report on Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE); ii. Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP), if there are unserviceable properties found during inventory taking; iii.     Request for Relief from Accountability by Accountable persons for lost PPE items under their accountability, if any; iv. Journal Entry Vouchers drawn by the Accounting Unit for recognition of loss of PPE from concerned accountable officers/ personnel, if any, and other necessary adjusting entries; and b.       Henceforth, enjoin the GSO designate and the Municipal Accountant to prepare and timely update property stock cards and ledger cards, respectively, and conduct regular reconciliation of records.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee has already drafted the Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) and stated therein the specific dates when the physical count of PPEs be conducted where Feb 19, 2024 is the start of the inventory taking. They also targeted that their Submission of the Report on Physical Count of PPEs be on March 31, 2024 and IIRUP on June 30, 2024
observations.reason: The Inventory Committee did not meet their target dates for the implementation of their PIP.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management instruct the Municipal Accountant to make necessary adjusting entry to effect the dropping of PPE costing below P50,000 pursuant to COA Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022, and provide the designated GSO the Journal Entry Voucher together with the schedule or list of affected PPE for his appropriate action.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant has made appropriate adjustments to comply with the new capitalization threshold for PPEs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management: 1. Instruct the municipal accountant to draw a correcting entry for transactions misclassified under the AP account; and 2. Require the municipal accountant to append the DR and IAR for the subject payables; otherwise, draw a reversing entry.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant has drawn adjustments to properly classified the payables. Delivery Receipts and Inspection and Acceptance Reports were appended to the JEV on the recognition of Payables.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management: a. Enjoin the Municipal Assessor to undertake necessary means to resolve the issues of duplication and overlapping of tax declarations to provide reliable assessment records; b. Thereafter, direct the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Municipal Assessor, to update the Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayers' index card and furnish the Municipal Accountant the required duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year; and c. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to draw adjusting entries to establish accurate receivable balances from RPT/SET and related accounts.

We recommended that management direct the Municipal Accountant to present the correction of prior period adjustments/errors by restating the comparative amounts for the period(s) presented in which adjustments/errors occurred, whenever practicable, and ensure adequate disclosures thereof in compliance with IPSAS 3.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant disclosed the details of the Prior Period Errors in the NTFS.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management: a) Enjoin the Acting Municipal Treasurer to comply the provisions as mandated under Section 254 in the Local Government Code for the posting requirement of the Notice of Delinquency as a condition sine qua non on availing judicial or administrative remedies which the Provincial Treasury Office may pursue; and b) Conduct extensive tax campaigns so that taxpayers are apprised of their rights and obligations incidental to their ownership of real properties including consequences on non-payment of RPT/SET.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer has complied with the posting requirement for tax delinquencies in three conspicuous areas of the Municipality, as mandated by the Local Government Code. Aside from sending demand letters to the delinquent taxpayers themselves, the Municipal Treasurer has conducted extensive tax campaigns during barangay assemblies to inform the taxpayers of their obligation to pay real property taxes.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management enjoin the Municipal Development Council through the MPDO to ensure that development projects are well-planned and are procurement-and-implementation-ready.
observations.reason: Except for the Loan Amortization for the year 2023, all infra projects were not implemented as scheduled.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that management: a.       Instruct the LDRRM Council to include all the unexpended prior year balances in its LDRRMF Investment Plan; b.       Direct the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer to: i.     Ensure the optimum utilization of the LDRRM Fund; ii.     Refrain from charging unrelated programs, projects and activities of disaster risk reduction and management and adhere to the provisions of the NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013; c. Direct the Bids and Awards Committee through the BAC Secretariat to ensure that the Notice of Award, Purchase Order/Contract of Agreement, and Notice to Proceed, for future procurement through any of the alternative modes, are posted in the PhilGEPS website as provided under Section 54.3 of IRR of RA No. 9184; and d. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to transfer the unutilized balances of the QRF and DRRMF-MOOE to the DRRM Special Trust Fund pursuant to Item 5.1.10 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 and draw journal entry treating such transfer.
observations.managementAction: a.       The management has passed a SB Resolutions for the unexpended LDRRMF balance. b.i.     The Municipality registered 80% utilization rate for the LGU’s current year DRRMF. b.ii.     The management has no longer charged unrelated expenditures.
observations.reason: The Management alleged that it is not possible to post Notice of Award, Purchase Order/Contract of Agreement, and Notice to Proceed for procurement through any of the alternative modes in the PhilGEPS website. However, no proof was presented to validate the claim.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management require the prospective suppliers to indicate brand names together with their relevant specifications during the filling out of the Requests for Quotations (RFQs) and ensure that brand names together with their complete detailed item specifications to be procured from the winning supplier are indicated in the Purchase Orders in compliance with COA Circular No. 96-010.
observations.managementAction: Items in the Purchase Order are already described with particularity, specifically for brand names.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management instruct the Municipal Engineer to include as a pay item the Project Billboard in the Approved Budget for the Contract and require the contractor to install project billboards at the project sites containing complete project information as soon as the award has been made in accordance with COA Circular 2013-004. Should the contractor fail to install project billboard, deduct its cost from the billing if the same is a pay item.
observations.managementAction: Recent Program of Works for projects already included Project Signboards as part of the pay items.

observations.recommendation: We recommend the management to enjoin the Municipal Accountant to ensure complete supporting documents for the RATA payment as required under COA Circular No. 2012-001 and strictly observe the prescribed rates of RATA in accordance with LBC No. 103 dated May 15, 2013.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant ensured that RATA claims are already supported with complete documentation and strictly complied with the rates.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that management: 1.       Submit a justification (a) highlighting the necessity of conducting the ELA Planning outside the locality when it could have been more efficiently undertaken within the locality and (b) for the attendance of non-members and uninvited officials and employees to PhALGA Annual National Conference CY 2022; and 2.       Enjoin the Local Chief Executive to strongly advocate to the principle of the DILG in conducting future similar activities within the geographical island where the municipality is located and in adherence to the principle of prudent use of scarce financial resources of the government.
observations.managementAction: The LGU has submitted written justification which we find it reasonable with the condition that the same will not transpire again. The LGU, thru the Local Chief Executive, has assured that they will measures to use LGU fund with due prudence and diligence.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management strictly comply with the provisions of EO 77 and stop the practice of paying travel expenses more than the amount authorized under the aforementioned EO.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality is now compliant with the provisions of EO 77.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that management instruct the GAD Focal Point System to:

1.       Ensure that the planned GAD activities are implemented as scheduled;
2.       In coordination with the Local Planning and Development Office, create a GAD database to include gender statistics and other relevant sex-disaggregated data, as basis in GAD planning, programming and policy-making; and
3.       Timely submit to the Audit Team the GAD Accomplishment Report.
observations.managementAction: The LGU endeavored to implement all their programmed GAD PPAs. However, based on the Accomplishment Report submitted, some PPAs were not accomplished.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management direct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Municipal Engineer on the status of projects recorded under the CIP accounts and reclassify the completed projects to its appropriate PPE accounts duly supported with Certificates of Project Completion.  Completed projects recorded under the Trust Fund shall be transferred to the General Fund upon submission of full liquidation to the grantor.   Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant shall reclassify projects recorded under the CIP to its appropriate PPE account upon completion of the project.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant has prepared adjusting entries to reflect reclassification of CIP account to its appropriate PPE accounts.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that management:

a.       Require the OIC-MAO and MNAO to submit to the Municipal Treasurer/GSO and the Municipal Accountant the Acknowledgement Receipts of the donations they have received and the list of beneficiaries with whom the donations were distributed, copy furnished the Office of the Auditor.
observations.reason: Due to renovation of the MAO’s building last CY 2021, some of the distribution lists can no longer be located; but the MAO has assured that the seedlings were fully distributed and utilized by the beneficiaries and that subsequent similar transactions were already documented as required.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management strictly impose liquidated damages to defaulting contractors/suppliers for future procurement projects in congruence with the provisions on liquidated damages under R.A. 9184.
observations.managementAction: The management has assigned personnel to track delays from the implemented projects. The contractors have requested Time Extensions and Suspension Orders subject to the approval of the Municipal Mayor.

observations.recommendation: We have reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation recommended that management direct the LDRRMO to include and implement in the LDRRM Investment Plan the deficiencies noted relative to compliance on the minimum standards for disaster preparedness. Likewise, we enjoined collaborative discussion thereon with the Sangguniang Bayan on the implementation and augmentation of the LDRRM Fund, if need be.
observations.managementAction: The LDRRMO has substantially complied with the minimum standards for disaster preparedness. In fact, the LGU was awarded during the Gawad Kalasag for the year 2023 for its full compliance on the standards for DRRM.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management consider the following: 1.      Direct the management to prioritize and to fast tract the completion of the LGU’s P/P/As mentioned above; 2.       Direct all officials and employees responsible for the conduct of procurement activity, including the end-users, to meticulously and judiciously plan the procurement; 3.       Direct the management to send a written demand to the supplier/contractor for the full and satisfactory performance of their obligations under their respective contract with the municipal government; 4.       Direct the management to enforce the provisions in the contract particularly the aspect on liquidated damages in congruence with the provisions on liquidated damages under R.A. 9184; and 5.       Evaluate the LGU’s capability to undertake projects by administration and take appropriate actions.
observations.managementAction: 1. The pertinent projects were already fully implemented except for the CY 2016 SALINTUBIG and CY2017 Core Local Road which were returned to the Bureau of Treasury. 2. (Reiterated with modification on this year's Audit Report in Observation No. 5) 3. The Municipal Engineer had sent demand letters to the contractors for the completion of the pertinent projects. 4. The Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant ensured enforcement of the liquidated damages, if warranted. 5. The Management has already considered their capability on undertaking projects by administration before resorting to such option on project implementation.

observations.recommendation: We have reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that management: 1.       Requiring the Municipal Collectors to demand a duly approved Requisition Slip from the customers/private contractors before processing rental payments; 2.       Instructing the Motorpool In-Charge to approve time records indicated in trip tickets filled-up by customers; 3.       Directing the Municipal Engineer/ Municipal Agriculturist, and the   GSO to conduct pre and post-inspection for every lease transaction; 4.       Requiring the GSO to verify details of trip tickets vis-à-vis the amount paid and require the contractor for payment of deficiencies, if there’s any; 5.       Instructing the GSO to maintain custody of equipment keys; 6.       Executing a lease contract with a contractor to cover the terms and conditions of the lease agreement; 7.      Instructing concerned offices to maintain equipment logbooks; and 8.       Directing the Municipal Treasurer to set income targets and prepare separate income reporting for monitoring purposes.
observations.managementAction: 1. The Collectors required Requisition Slip from the lessees before processing rental payments. 2. Not Implemented - There was no formal designation for Motorpool-in-Charge who will act based on the recommendation. 3. Not Implemented - No concrete action from the concerned Officials to address the recommendation. 4. Not Implemented - No concrete action from the designated GSO on this regard. 5. Not Implemented - The keys are still in the custody of the drivers. 6. Not Implemented - Still, no lease contracts were executed to formalize lease of heavy equipment. 7. Fully Implemented - The Municipal Collector designated to monitor the heavy equipment rental already maintains logbook to monitor release and return of the equipment. 8. Not Implemented - This was not considered by the Municipal Treasurer during the budget preparation for the LGU.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following:

The Local Chief Executive to direct the concerned employees to gather necessary papers/documents and facilitate the transfer of ownership of the donated vehicles to the Municipality; and
Sangguniang Bayan to consider the need to revise the Market Code 2012 to increase the rates of fees and charges.

We also recommended that the offices concerned conduct an inventory of all the parcels of land owned by the Municipality and reconcile with the records in the books for proper presentation in the Financial Statements.

We recommended the following:

a. The LEE to refund the amount of Fund Transfers to the General Fund of the Municipality;

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
Direct the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer to facilitate the preparation of the PPE Ledger Cards and Property Cards, respectively, and reconcile the same, in accordance with Sections 119 and 120, Chapter 7 of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual;
Provide additional manpower, when feasible, to assist in the preparation and reconciliation of PPE records and reports;
Instruct the GSO and the Inventory Committee to:
Prepare the Report of the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) based on the capitalization threshold of PPE of P50,000.00, as provided in COA Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022;
Prepare the RPCPPE per type of PPE as stated in NGAS Volume II Instructions for Annex 49-RPCPPE; and
Make the necessary corrections on the errors and inaccuracies in the CY 2022 RPCPPE and submit the corrected report to the Office of the Auditor.
Direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Make the necessary adjustments to restate the PPE balance following the capitalization threshold for PPE of ₱50,000.00, in accordance with Item 4.2 of COA Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022; and
Strictly follow the recording of a tangible item as PPE, if and only if, it meets the definition and recognition criteria for PPE, which includes the capitalization threshold of ₱50,000.00.
Require the Inventory Committee, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, to:
Prioritize the preparation of the approved Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) and submit it to the Office of the Auditor, at least ten (10) calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory-taking activities; and
Conduct the one-time cleansing of the PPE to establish the correct value of the account.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct:
The Municipal Treasurer to:
Ensure accuracy and completeness in the preparation of the Report of Checks Issued in accordance with Section 43 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs; and
Prepare and provide a copy of the list of unreleased checks to the Municipal Accountant at the end of the year.
The Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjustments for unreleased checks at year-end, to restore the Cash in Bank and corresponding Payable accounts in the Financial Statements.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct:
The Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Municipal Assessor, to prepare and furnish the Municipal Accountant at the beginning of the year with a duly certified list of taxpayers showing the amount due and collectible for the year;
The Municipal Accountant to:
Recognize the year-end balance of the receivable accounts in the books based on the Certified List of Real Property Tax Delinquencies report from the Municipal Treasurer; and
Coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer for the timely submission of the certified list of taxpayers and eventual recognition in the books at the beginning of the calendar year of the Real Property Tax/Special Education Tax Receivables and related Deferred accounts, in accordance with Section 20 of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Volume I for LGU; and
The Municipal Treasurer to create a more aggressive and intensified campaign for the collection of all RPT and SET Receivables and consequently, to possibly minimize uncollected accounts.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct:
The Municipal Accountant to follow the Perpetual Inventory Method in the accounting of inventories in compliance with Section 114 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for LGUs Volume I; and
The GSO to ensure that issuance of supplies and materials are supported with Requisition and Issue Slip and prepare and submit the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued to the Municipal Accountant for the recording of expense.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Accountant to:
Expedite the reconciliation of the documents relative to the abnormal/negative balances in the subsidiary ledgers of the Due to NGAs and Due to LGUs accounts with a total debit balance of ₱7.986 million; and
Prepare the necessary adjusting entries in accordance with Item 7.3 of COA Circular No. 2016-005, to reflect in the financial statements the correct balance of the Due to NGAs and Due to LGUs accounts.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Exhaust efforts to retrieve the supporting documents and expedite the return of the unexpended balances to the Source Agencies in accordance with 
COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994; and
Coordinate with the concerned implementing offices upon receipt of funds transferred from various NGAs and LGUs, for the timely implementation of the said offices of the programs, projects and activities, and prompt recording of its subsequent liquidation.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct:
The Accountant to:
Require completeness of the attached DTTs for fuel disbursements; and
Ensure consistency of data between the Charge Invoices and Statements of Account, Charge Invoices and Gas Tickets, and Gas Tickets and DTTs.
The Municipal Treasurer to ensure that all disbursements are duly supported with proof of payment such as official receipt or its equivalent document, that should contain complete details of payment including the name of payee, date of payment, particulars and amount; and
The Responsible Officer, in accordance with COA Circular No. 77-61, dated September 26, 1977, to:
Ensure that Driver’s Trip Tickets are approved, properly accomplished, serially numbered, and duly signed by the driver and the passenger;
Prepare the Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption in accordance with the format prescribed in Annex G of the Manual on Audit for Fuel Consumption of Government Vehicles and submit to the Office of the Auditor; and
Prepare the Monthly Report of Official Travels and submit it to the Office of the Auditor.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
Direct the Accountant to devise ways and means in order to prepare the necessary reports readily and accurately, without resorting to misclassification of the expenses;
Instruct the concerned offices, when necessary, to provide a specific budget for Fuel, Oil and Lubricants, otherwise, undergo the correct process of budget augmentation; and
Require the Accountant to stop the practice of recording the fuel expenses in accounts other than Fuel, Oil and Lubricants to strictly adhere to COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the designated Property Officer to accomplish and file the Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE) form for all of the PPE issued in accordance with Section 123, Chapter 7 of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual, in order to determine the accountable persons and establish accountability.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the 
Municipal Accountant to:
Exhaust all efforts to retrieve the supporting documents relative to the long outstanding book reconciling items of the General and Trust Funds; and
Cause the immediate preparation of the journal entries to record the reconciling items that require adjustment and correction on the GL in accordance with Section 3.3 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant:
To prepare the bank reconciliation statements for the Bottom Up Budgeting and KALAHI Trust Fund Accounts in order to determine the reconciling items for the net difference of ₱27,859.15 between the book balance and the bank confirmation balance, in compliance with Section 74 of P.D. No. 1445 and Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 6, 1996;
To prepare journal entry vouchers to record all valid reconciling items that require adjustment and correction in the GL consistent with Section 3.3 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 6, 1996; and
To submit the copies of the BRS to the Auditor within ten (10) days from receipt of BS after the end of each month, including the paid checks, original copies of the debit and credit memo in accordance with Section 3.4 of COA Circular
No. 96-011 dated October 6, 1996.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the MDRRMO:
To submit monthly to the Auditor the Report on Utilization of the DRRMF on or before the 15th day after the end of each month in accordance with Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012; and
To furnish the Office of the Civil Defense (OCD) and the Department of the Interior and Local Government Operations Office (DILG-OO) a copy of the LDRRMFIP and the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF in compliance with Section 5.1.6 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated 
September 12, 2012.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the MDRRMO to ensure strict compliance with Section 4 of NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC 
No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 on the utilization of the DRRMF.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct:
The MDRRMO, together with the Municipal Development Council, to review and update the LDRRMFIP annually to include a list of specific projects and activities to be charged against the LDRRMF of prior years and incorporate it in the AIP; and
The OIC-Municipal Accountant to ensure that the PPAs are included in the AIP prior to the processing of disbursements related to its implementation.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting journal entries and comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 by following the prescribed accounting entries in order to produce a more accurate presentation of the Financial Statements.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct:
The concerned Rural Health Unit personnel to prepare the Inventory Report on the remaining undistributed vaccines as of year-end with information as to valuation and furnish the copy to the Office of the Municipal Accountant; and
The Municipal Accountant to record the transaction on the agency books based on the complete Inventory Report in compliance with COA Circular 
No. 2020-009.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Accountable Officer to stamp “PAID” the Disbursement Vouchers (DVs) and all the supporting documents attached to the DVs after the payment is made, to prevent re-use of the said documents for similar transactions.

We reiterated, and the management agreed on the recommendation that the Local Chief Executive or his representative, propose to the Sangguniang Bayan to revise/update the Market Code and adjust accordingly the fees and charges, consistent with Municipal Ordinance No. 2017-19 or the Revised Revenue Code of the Municipality, to increase its resources.

We reiterated, and the management agreed on the following recommendations:
The Local Chief Executive to direct the concerned employees or designate personnel to gather the necessary papers/documents and facilitate the transfer of ownership of the donated vehicles to the Municipality; and
The Municipal Accountant to record the donated equipment in the books of account upon completion of the necessary documents to support the Municipality’s ownership of the assets consistent with International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) No. 23.

We reiterated, and the Local Chief Executive agreed on the recommendation to direct:
The Municipal Assessor’s Office to expedite the titling of all parcels of land owned by the Municipal Government in compliance with Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386 and furnish the Municipal Accountant of the supporting documents for recording purposes;
The Inventory Committee to conduct an inventory of all the parcels of land owned by the Municipality consistent with Section 5.1 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 and reconcile with the records in the books; and
The Municipal Accountant to prepare necessary journal entries to reflect the correct balance of the Land account in the financial statements.

We reiterated, and the management agreed on the recommendation that the LEE exhaust efforts to increase its collections and/or adopt cost-cutting measures to produce funds for the refund of the amount of fund transfers of ₱8.885 million to the General Fund of the Municipality.

We recommended and the management agreed that:

The Inventory Committee submit the RPCPPE to the accounting office before the preparation of the FS to effect necessary adjustments on the PPE balance;

The Municipal Accountant and GSO maintain the required PPE Ledger Cards and Property Cards, respectively, and reconcile their property records; and

(AAR 2020 Finding No. 1)





The Municipality avail of the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts as provided under Commission on Audit (COA) Circular No. 2020-006 to finally establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to exert effort to identify to which the receivables pertain and determine the possibility of collection and make necessary accounting entries to correct the balance of the receivable.



We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to require the Municipal Treasurer to submit a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year before recognizing Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax.

Management to conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis and validation of the cash advances, determine long outstanding and dormant receivables and prepare necessary reports together with the supporting documents for the request for write-off, if warranted, as indicated in COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the: 

MDRRMO to submit monthly to the Auditor the Report on Utilization of the DRRMF; and

(AAR 2014, Finding No. 9)




Office of the Municipal Accountant to include a discussion on the details of the unexpended balances of the MDRRMF in the Notes to Financial Statements as required in Annex D of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended and the management agreed that the Municipal Planning and Development Council and the OIC-Municipal Budget Officer only include those programs, projects and activities in Annual Investment Plan that are allowable under DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 which shall partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures.

We recommended and the Management and the supplier agreed to comply with the provision of the contract.


We also recommended and the Management agreed to require the supplier to provide complete information and documentation to support its claim before payment.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the General Services Officer or responsible personnel to submit within five working days from execution/issuance the Purchase Order and all the supporting documents for review of the Auditor.

Management to conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis and validation of the cash advances, determine long outstanding and dormant receivables and prepare necessary reports together with the supporting documents for the request for write-off, if warranted, as indicated in COA Circular No. 2016-005.

See to it that the duties and responsibilities/scope of work of each JO are stipulated in a specific manner in the contract and make sure that all JOs sign in it confirming/agreeing with all the terms and conditions.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

Instruct the concerned officials and employees to communicate with the MLGOO and DILG Regional Office regarding the Copy of the List of Eligible LGUs and the Notice of Eligibility;
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to see to it that all disbursements are supported with complete documentation

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Accounting Office:
Prepare a working paper describing the assets recorded in the PPE         account with an acquisition cost of less than P15,000.00, including the supporting documents such as the Property Acknowledgment Receipt (PAR) of said assets and other relevant data.  Draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) in order to make an adjusting entry to remove the same from the account “Property, Plant, and Equipment”. All these will be submitted to the Office of the Auditor for verification; and
Subsequent purchases of equipment and other items with acquisition cost of less than P15,000.00 shall  be recorded as inventory upon purchase and expense upon issuance with the corresponding Inventory Custodian Slip (ICS) to establish accountability over assets.

We have recommended that management direct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Municipal Engineer on the status of projects recorded under the CIP accounts and reclassify the completed projects to its appropriate PPE accounts duly supported with Certificates of Project Completion.  Completed projects recorded under the Trust Fund shall be transferred to the General Fund upon submission of full liquidation to the grantor.   Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant shall reclassify projects recorded under the CIP to its appropriate PPE account upon completion of the project.

We recommend that management:
Direct the concerned officials to immediately settle their                     accountabilities through the submission of necessary supporting         documents and to refund any excess amount of cash advances;
Follow up the demand letters issued if any; and
Request for authority to write off dormant unliquidated cash              advances which remained non-moving for ten (10) years or more and when settlement could no longer be ascertained in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that management direct the Municipal Engineer and the contractors to submit the above-mentioned lacking requirements for future infrastructure projects as a pre-requisite prior to payment of final billings pursuant to COA Circular 2012-001.

We have recommended that management:
Streamline and document the donation policy and process thru the             issuance of an Executive Order or a Memorandum, which among others, would contain the employees authorized to receive donations and their reportorial responsibilities; and
Require the OIC-MAO and MNAO to submit to the Municipal Treasurer/GSO and the Municipal Accountant the Acknowledgement Receipts of the donations they have received and the list of beneficiaries with whom the donations were distributed, copy furnished the Office of the Auditor.

We have recommended that management:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the payee listed in the DV is the client or his/her immediate family member.  If for any reason, the client or his/her family member cannot make the claim personally, require the submission of an authorization letter; and
Ensure complete documentation in the General Intake Sheet especially for the social worker’s assessment and the recommended service and     assistance as basis for the preparation of the Obligation Request.

We have recommended that management strictly impose liquidated damages to defaulting contractors/suppliers for future procurement projects in congruence with the provisions on liquidated damages under R.A. 9184.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:
the Municipal Accountant to (i) exert extra effort to account for the unreconciled difference appearing in the bank statements and financial statements and to faithfully present the correct balances of the accounts;  (ii) conduct a thorough review of the affected accounts to determine the cause of abnormal balances, to initiate corrections/adjustments and thereafter come up with all accounts in their normal balances; and (iii) reconcile the disparity between the balances of the reciprocal accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds and present the correct balances of intra-agency receivables and payables in the financial statements; and
the Municipal Treasurer to inquire from the depository banks for the possible enrollment of the agency’s accounts, with the former’s websites which would allow the electronic generation of bank statements/statement of accounts at any given period of time, for the Municipal Accountant to be able to timely prepare the BRS and submit the same to the Auditor within the prescribed period.
With regard to recommendation b above, the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer signified their compliance.  They also mentioned that the recommendations provided by the Audit Team are appreciated as it could address the issue on the delayed preparation of BRS.

We reiterate our previous years’ audit recommendations that the Municipal Mayor:
direct the Acting Municipal Treasurer/Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of all the inventory items and property, plant and equipment of the Municipality at year-end to determine the validity and reliability of existence of its inventories and properties.  The committee shall reconcile the results of the count with the property records and accounting records and submit the complete and accurate RPCI and RPCPPE to the Audit Team, pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.   Coordinated efforts of the employees should be encouraged to be able to submit the required reports.
Also, the above concerned personnel are advised to avail of the prescribed guidelines/procedures set forth under COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 relative to the one-time cleansing of the enormous amount of discrepancies in the PPE account balances of the Municipality.
It is to be emphasized, however, that the circular was issued for one-time cleansing of PPE account balances; thus, in no case shall the procedures be used to further derecognize non-existing/missing PPEs and/or cleanse subsequent discrepancies or unreconciled balances in PPE accounts;
direct the Municipal Accountant to effect transfer of completed projects for LGU use from the Trust Fund to the General Fund and reclassify the Investment Property – Land account to reflect the correct PPE balances in compliance with the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs per COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 and Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1.  The following are the suggested accounts to be used in recording the transaction relative to the mangrove reforestation project of the Municipality, viz;
and
require the Property Custodian and Accounting Unit to maintain property cards and PPE ledger cards, respectively, indicating the details quantities, exact date of acquisition, and status/condition of all PPEs and conduct periodic reconciliation to bring both records into agreement to achieve a timely and reliable financial report/statements.
For inventory items, maintenance of the supplies ledger cards by the Municipal Accountant and stock cards by the Treasurer or Property Custodian should be required to ensure a check and balance in the issuance of supplies and materials.

We reiterated our prior years’ audit recommendations that the Municipal Mayor:
for cash advances of accountable officers who are still connected with the agency, require immediate settlement of their outstanding cash advances.  Failure on the part of accountable officers and employees to settle/liquidate their cash advances shall constitute a valid ground for the withholding of the payment of any money due them;
for unsettled accounts of personnel who are already deceased, exhaust all remedies to collect or demand to settle the unliquidated cash advances, otherwise, apply for write off in accordance with the guidelines and procedures prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 29, 2016; and
instruct the Municipal Accountant to properly classify each cash advance transaction in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 and properly classify long outstanding cash advances in the financial statements in accordance with paragraphs 70 and 76 of IPSAS I.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
instruct the BAC to (i) furnish the Audit Team with a copy of the PO and its attachments within five working days upon issuance thereof as required under Section 3.2.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009; and (ii)  submit the lacking documents and/or written explanation on the deficiencies noted and henceforth, strictly observe the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 to ensure adherence to the principles of transparency, competitiveness and public monitoring; and
require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to return the unexpended balance of the BGCM to its respective source agencies as stipulated in Item 5 of DBM Circular Letter No. 2021-13, and secure Official Receipt for the same in compliance with Items 4.9 and 6.7 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994.

We recommended and the management agreed that:
The Inventory Committee submit the RPCPPE to the accounting office before the preparation of the FS to effect necessary adjustments on the PPE balance;
The Municipal Accountant and GSO maintain the required PPE Ledger Cards and Property Cards, respectively, and reconcile their property records; and
The Municipality avail of the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts as provided under Commission on Audit (COA) Circular No. 2020-006 to finally establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to exert effort to identify to which the receivables pertain and determine the possibility of collection and make necessary accounting entries  to correct the balance of the receivable.
We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to require the Municipal Treasurer to submit a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year before recognizing Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the previous Collection Officers to produce the undeposited collections and deposit the same with the Authorized Government Depository Bank (AGDB) and provide explanation why the said amount was undeposited as of December 31, 2021 or if necessary, initiate proper proceedings.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the:
MDRRMO to submit monthly to the Auditor the Report on Utilization of the DRRMF; and
Office of the Municipal Accountant to include a discussion on the details of the unexpended balances of the MDRRMF in the Notes to Financial Statements as required in Annex D of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended and the management agreed that the Municipal Planning and Development Council and the OIC-Municipal Budget Officer only include those programs, projects and activities in Annual Investment Plan that are allowable under DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 which shall partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures.

We recommended and the Management and the supplier agreed to comply with the provision of the contract.
We also recommended and the Management agreed to require the supplier to provide complete information and documentation to support its claim before payment.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the General Services Officer or responsible personnel to submit within five working days from execution/issuance the Purchase Order and all the supporting documents for review of the Auditor.

We have recommended that management:

Instruct the Supply Officer to prepare and submit a complete inventory report to account the undistributed relief goods;

Direct the concerned Offices to validate the completeness of the Distribution Lists and submit the same to the audit team.  Any discrepancy should be satisfactorily justified by the Management; and

Establish clear procedures or flow of the relief operations including proper authorization and ensure that these are properly disseminated

 to avoid confusion or any misunderstanding in the procurement and distribution of relief goods.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that management:

Organize a validation team who will work in close coordination with barangay officials concerned and the DSWD to conduct verification on the eligibility of the aforementioned beneficiaries based on the filled-up SAC;

Direct the validation team to submit to the audit team a report/status of their verification and the actions taken by management; and 

Confer with DSWD-Regional Office No. 10 on the results of the revalidation and obtain a written advise as to the course of action if recipients were declared to be ineligible.
observations.managementAction:
On March 10, 2020, the Municipal Mayor issued Memorandum Order No. 005 organizing the validation team to conduct verification on the eligibility of beneficiaries based on the SAC forms.

On April 29, 2021 the following documents were submitted to COA office:
Barangay Certification
SAP beneficiaries data information
Attached pictures for the new beneficiaries receiving SAP coming from those returned by the beneficiaries
Letter to DSWD Director/cc: COA
observations.reason:
Fully        Implemented

Of the seven (7) duplicate beneficiaries presented in the audit finding, five (5) were confirmed as duplicate beneficiaries during the revalidation conducted by the LGU. The amounts received by these five (5) duplicate beneficiaries were returned  and given directly to new beneficiaries identified as replacement.

Also, of the 82 beneficiaries presented in the audit finding with no listed family members, 81 were found to have family members who were erroneously not reflected in the SAC forms, as per revalidation conducted by the LGU.
Meanwhile, one beneficiary was found to be a 4Ps beneficiary, hence the amount he received was returned and given to another beneficiary identified as replacement.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that management:

Require the Municipal Treasurer to strictly:

Indicate control/reference numbers in the RCDs including the date of receipt of remittance from the Liquidating Officer;

Record each transaction in the cashbook meticulously according to the dates of actual collections and deposits; and

Ensure that all transactions are completely and correctly recorded in the cashbook.


2. Require the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to immediately reconcile their records to avoid further accumulation of errors/reconciling items.  Thereafter, observe the necessary monthly reconciliation for early detection of errors and/or omissions of transactions.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Treasurer had instructed the liquidating officer to comply and indicate the control/reference number of the RCD including the date of receipt of remittances as per COA recommendations.

The Municipal Treasurer/Liquidating Officer complied with the COA recommendations to meticulously record each transactions according to the dates of actual collections and deposits to ensure complete and correct recording in the cashbook.

The Municipal Treasurer and Accountant had its periodic reconciliation of cash balances, for this will be the tool in the preparation and submission of ESRE report.
observations.reason: Cash balances for SEF account are not yet reconciled.

observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer monitored/checked the collections and remittances of every collectors/Liquidating Officer for detection of errors or omission of recordings of the RCD and cashbook.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that management provide legal basis citing that the said payment of honoraria to the MBOC shall be sourced from the LGU fund and submit a certificate of non-payment of honoraria from the COMELEC Central Office Accountant.  Otherwise, bill the COMELEC for the total election expenses incurred during the May 13, 2019 National and Local Elections in accordance with Section 10 of the Omnibus Election Code.

Revised recommendation:
Pursuant to COMELEC Minute Resolution No. 19-1054, submit  justification that the honoraria granted to the Municipal Board of Canvassers during the May 13, 2019 National and Local Elections is not similar to the honorarium or allowance given by the COMELEC, otherwise the payment shall be disallowed in audit.

COMELEC Minute Resolution No. 07-0626 dated March 27, 2007 is superseded by COMELEC Minute Resolution No. 19-1054 dated October 30, 2019, which resolved that the Sangguniang Bayan, SangguniangPanlungsod,  SangguniangPanlalawigan of municipalities, cities and provinces, respectively:

1.	May continue to grant an honorarium or allowance to the members of the municipal/city/provincial board of canvassers for the May 13, 2019 National and Local Elections and all subsequent electoral exercises; such honorarium or allowance must not be similar to the honorarium or allowance being given by this Commission; and

2.	May provide in their respective ordinances that of the recipient is already receiving a similar honorarium or allowance for the same service or period, respectively, for which payment is being claimed, the recipient may choose to  receive whichever is higher, but in no case must the recipient receive both amounts.

Hence, the audit team revised the audit recommendation.
observations.managementAction: The LGU already submitted a justification that the honoraria granted by the LGU to the Municipal Board of Canvassers during the May 13, 2019 National and Local Elections was for services rendered days before and after the election day while the Honorarium granted by the COMELEC covers only the payment of actual services rendered during the day of the elections.
observations.reason:
Fully
Implemented

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Management agreed that the (a) BAC (i) submit the required
documents such as SPA, Secretary’s Certificate or Board/Partnership Resolution, as the
case may be, evidencing authority of the supplier’s purported representative to act on
behalf of his/her principal and ensure compliance of the same in future procurement
contracts; (b) Accounting Office submit the list of patients admitted to the quarantine
facilities and persons vaccinated so that transactions involved can be further re-evaluated;
and (c) BAC and concerned officials of the agency comply with the requirements under
the 2016 Revised IRRs of R.A. No. 9184 and submit the lacking documentary requirements
to the Audit Team to prove the propriety and regularity of disbursement transactions noted.

We reiterated our previous recommendations and the Management agreed to
i) conduct a complete annual physical count of Inventories to come up with RPCI with
its related reconciliation as required in Section 124 of the MNGAS for LGUs; and ii)
maintain SLCs and SCs and observe periodic reconciliation of the two records for
proper monitoring of agency assets.

We recommended and the Management agreed that (a) the MGSO, together
with the Inventory Committee, (i) conduct physical count of PPE and prepare the
corresponding RPCPPE; (ii) allot time to ask for the PPE records maintained by the
previous property custodian; (b) the Accounting Office and GSO reconcile their
respective PPE records and the latter to update and maintain PCs following the
account classification prescribed for LGUs; (c) the Municipal Accountant allot time
and exert effort to look for the previous PPE records in order to provide a reliable
provision for depreciation of PPE; and (d) the Municipal Accountant, with the
assistance of the Assessor’s Office, determine the fair market value of the unrecorded
lands owned by the Municipality and book the same as part of PPE.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct (a) the Municipal Engineer
and other concerned officials to fast-track the completion of those partially
implemented and on-going projects and immediately implement remaining projects
under the 20% DF including those prior years’ unimplemented projects so that
expected outputs and outcomes will be realized; and (b) the Municipal Planning and
Development Office (MPDO) and other related offices (i) to ensure the proper
preparation of the APP by providing more specific schedule of procurement activities
based on identified priority projects; and (ii) to monitor and evaluate the timeliness
of the implementation of the PPAs to ensure that the delivery of socio-economic
services are within the timeframes set.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the (a) BAC (i) submit the
required documents such as SPA, Secretary’s Certificate or Board/Partnership
Resolution, as the case may be, evidencing authority of the supplier’s purported
representative to act on behalf of his/her principal and ensure compliance of the same
in future procurement contracts; (ii) submit new warranty security with correct
written warranty provision pertaining to goods and carefully examine and read
insurance contracts to prevent such errors in the future; (b) Accounting Office submit
the list persons vaccinated so that transactions involved can be further re-evaluated;
and (c) BAC and concerned officials of the agency comply with the requirements
under the 2016 Revised IRRs of R.A. No. 9184 and submit the lacking documentary
requirements to the Audit Team to prove the propriety and regularity of
disbursement transactions noted.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct (a) all concerned officials
and personnel (i) to strictly adhere to the provisions concerning the implementation of
projects funded by LGSF; and (ii) to submit the lacking documents to the Audit
Team for evaluation and examination and observe stringent compliance on the
submission of documentary requirements of transactions; and (b) the Municipal
Accountant to review thoroughly all claims presented for payment prior to approval to
ensure that only properly and completely documented transactions/disbursement
are paid.

We also recommended and the Management agreed to ensure that all lands
where the infrastructure are proposed to be constructed are government properties
to avoid delay in the realization of the projects.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the GFPS take the lead
and communicate with the DILG or with the PCW, or other competent government
agencies authorized to conduct GAD trainings for the purpose of requesting a
customized workshop on HGDG and PIMME tools.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer agreed to immediately prepare adjusting entries to effect reconciliation of balances under the PhilHealth Capitation Fund and Calamity Trust Fund accounts and attached supporting documents for proper verification by the Audit Team on the correctness of the adjustments made.  For the discrepancies noted under the General, Special Education and Trust Funds, exert extra effort to account for the unreconciled difference appearing in the bank statements and financial statements and to faithfully present the correct balances of the accounts.
observations.managementAction: Discrepancies noted under the Trust Fund with LBP Acct. No. 2032-1060-40 (Calamity) and 2032-1060-59 (PhilHealth) were already reconciled. The necessary adjustments for discrepancies noted in CY 2020 were already adjusted in CY 2021.
observations.reason: The unreconciled difference between the book and bank balance remained at P9,859,876.08 as at December 31, 2021.  The Accounting Office has yet to look for relevant documents to properly determine the causes of the discrepancies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to effect the necessary adjusting entries in the books of the agency to be able to present the accounts fairly in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Accounts with abnormal balances in CY 2020 such as Cash – Local Treasury, RPT Receivable, Due to BIR under the General and Trust Funds were restored to their normal balances.
observations.reason: The Special Education Tax Receivable and Deferred Special Education Tax accounts under the Special Education Fund still have a negative balance as at December 31, 2021.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor:

direct the Acting Municipal Treasurer/ Inventory Committee to initiate the conduct of the complete physical count of all the inventories and property, plant and equipment of the Municipality at year-end to determine the validity and reliability of existence of its inventories and properties.  The committee shall reconcile the results of the count with the property records and accounting records and submit the complete and accurate RPCI and RPCPPE to the Audit Team, pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.   Coordinated efforts of the employees should be encouraged to be able to submit the required reports.

In case of discrepancies in the PPE account balances between the result of the physical count and the accounting records, the Inventory Committee may refer to COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 which provides the Guidelines and Procedures in the Conduct of Physical Count of PPE, Recognition of PPE Items Found at Station, and Disposition for Non-existing/Missing PPE Items, for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances of Government Agencies; and

direct the Municipal Accountant to effect transfer of completed projects for LGU use from the Trust Fund to the General Fund so as to properly present PPE accounts in the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction:
The Inventory Committee has conducted a partial physical count of inventories and PPE of the Municipality for the year CY 2021.

The Report on partial Physical Count of PPEs for CY 2021 was submitted to the Audit Team on June 16, 2022.
observations.reason:
The Municipality still failed to conduct complete physical count of all the inventories and property, plant and equipment.

Moreover, the Municipality failed to reconcile the result of the physical count with the accounting records as at December 31, 2021.



















Same audit observations were noted for CY 2021 as discussed in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our prior years’ audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor:

for cash advances of accountable officers who are still connected with the agency, require immediate settlement of their outstanding cash advances.  Failure on the part of accountable officers and employees to settle/liquidate their cash advances shall constitute a valid ground for the withholding of the payment of any money due them;  

for unsettled accounts of personnel who are already deceased, exhaust all remedies to collect or demand to settle the unliquidated cash advances, otherwise, apply for write off in accordance with the guidelines and procedures prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 29, 2016; and

instruct the Municipal Accountant to properly classify each cash advance transaction in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 and properly classify long outstanding cash advances in the financial statements in accordance with paragraphs 70 and 76 of IPSAS 1.
observations.managementAction: The Local Government of Concepcion has already made a series of issuance of demand letters pertaining to unliquidated cash advances of various employees in CY 2021 which resulted to settlement and payment of P30,525.00.
observations.reason:
Cash advances totaling P482,462.54 which were already due remained unliquidated as at December 31, 2021.




















Cash advances in the amount of P21,318.89 and P6,727.00 pertaining to unsettled accounts of personnel who are already deceased and resigned, respectively, remained as at December 31, 2021.




















Cash advances outstanding for more than one year were still classified as current assets as at December 31, 2021.

observations.recommendation:
Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

require the MDRRMO and the Municipal Accountant to submit written explanation/justification and/or submit the required lacking documents to prove the regularity of the subject disbursements;

require the personnel in charge of the preparation of the Purchase Orders to ensure that instances wherein the suppliers have already signed in the conforme portion even prior to its issuance and deliveries of goods to the Municipality also even prior to the issuance of the POs are avoided; and

require the Municipal Accountant to (i) adhere strictly to the accounting and reporting guidelines for LDRRMF set forth in COA Circular No. 2012-002 specifically on the transfer of unutilized LDRRMF; (ii) see to it that all the necessary documentary requirements attached to the vouchers are accurate and complete before processing the claims; and (iii) prepare adjusting accounting entries to correct the deficiency noted in the recording of transactions.
observations.managementAction:
There were no more instances wherein the suppliers have already signed in the conforme portion of the Purchase Order even prior to its issuance in CY 2021.




The MDRRM Office has submitted its monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF to the audit team for CY 2021.

The Municipality was not able to submit a justification/ explanation letter relative to the audit findings as of this writing.





The utilization report for the months of January, February, April, October and November 2021 were not submitted to the Audit Team, while reports for the months of June, July and December 2021 were submitted beyond the prescribed period.

require the personnel in charge of the preparation of the Purchase Orders to ensure that instances wherein the suppliers have already signed in the conforme portion even prior to its issuance and deliveries of goods to the Municipality also even prior to the issuance of the POs are avoided and see to it that goods are delivered on time.  Imposed liquidated damages on the failure to deliver goods on the specific date indicated in the POs pursuant to Section 8 of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184; and

require the Municipal Accountant to (i) see to it that all the necessary documentary requirements attached to the vouchers are accurate and complete before processing the claims; and (ii) prepare adjusting accounting entries to correct the deficiency noted in the recording of transactions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant observe the provisions given in allowing and disallowing the travel expenses claimed by the officials and employees of the Municipality and see to it that disbursement vouchers are completely supported with valid documents before processing the claims.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2021, reimbursements of actual expenses were duly supported with certification from the Municipal Mayor as necessary in performance of the travel order.
observations.reason:
For CY 2021, there were instances wherein a certification of expenses not requiring receipts was not provided for expenses amounting to P300.00 or less.

Also, claims for taxi fare were not provided with an official receipt.

Further, certification for travel completed duly approved by the Municipal Mayor were not always accomplished.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to comply with the requirement of the perpetual inventory system wherein regular purchases shall be coursed thru the inventory account and issuances thereof shall be recorded as they take place except those purchased out of petty cash fund which shall be for immediate use and not for stock. Such case shall be charged immediately to the appropriate expense accounts. 

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer regularly prepare and submit accurate and complete Summary of Supplies and Materials Issue (SSMI) with supporting Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) pursuant to Section 121 of the NGAS Manual, Volume I, to the Accounting Unit for proper recording/posting of issuance in the books of accounts and Supplies Ledger Cards (SLCs).

On the other hand, we recommended the Municipal Accountant prepare JEVs to adjust/record issuances on the affected accounts and coordinate closely with the Municipal Treasurer to ensure reconciliation of their inventory records, also in respect of physical inventory taking.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2021, the Accounting Office has observed the perpetual inventory system in recording purchases.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to disclose the required information in the notes to financial statements and submit general ledger and subsidiary ledgers for all funds in accordance with the abovementioned regulations.

All nine barangays of the Municipality have already established their MRF.

Also, garbage collectors are not yet equipped with proper training on handling/dealing with garbage.

the Municipal Accountant to set up RPT/SET Receivables in accordance with Section 20 of NGAS Manual, Volume I. the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant at the beginning of the year with a duly certified list of Real Property Taxpayers showing the names of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year; and

the Municipal Assessor to furnish the Treasurer with the latest valuations of property of each taxpayer.

4.1 We recommended and the management agreed to strictly adhere to the provisions of the Revised IRR of the RA No. 9184 and pertinent guidelines provided in GPPB Resolutions No. 03-2020 and 05-2020 to efficiently conduct procurement activities, specifically during state of calamity.
